purpose: Track what works in Gemini image generation prompts. Build this over time. Each entry is a lesson.

style_modifiers.ALWAYS_APPEND: No text, no watermarks, no speech bubbles. Pure visual storytelling.
style_modifiers.CINEMATIC: Dramatic camera angle. Cinematic composition. Rule of thirds.
style_modifiers.DETAIL: Hyper-detailed rendering. Every surface, every shadow, razor sharp.
style_modifiers.NOIR_LIGHT: Single strong light source. Deep shadows. Chiaroscuro.

what_works.lesson: Specifying '40-hour detail density' and 'obsessive crosshatching' triggers McFarlane-level rendering more than 'detailed'
what_works.example: spawn-matrix-v2 prompts — explicit instruction about spending 40 hours inking

what_works.lesson: UPPERCASE color names in prompts get more attention from Gemini — 'CRIMSON' vs 'crimson'
what_works.example: v2 uses CRIMSON, ELECTRIC BLUE, TOXIC GREEN, HELLFIRE ORANGE — more vivid than lowercase descriptions

what_works.lesson: Explicitly saying 'panel borders ACTIVELY BROKEN' and 'tendrils CROSS THE PANEL BORDER' triggers the bleeding-panel effect
what_works.example: McFarlane's signature — cape/energy that refuses to be contained by the grid

what_works.lesson: Naming competing light sources by color creates more interesting facial lighting than 'dramatic lighting'
what_works.example: 'CRIMSON from left, ELECTRIC BLUE from right, TOXIC GREEN in eyes' > 'dramatic screen glow'

what_works.lesson: Specifying a real artist's name (McFarlane, Kirby, Moebius, Mucha) produces more recognizable style than describing the style abstractly
what_works.example: 'Todd McFarlane Spawn art style' > 'dark hyper-detailed comic art'

what_works.lesson: Giving AI a color temperature conflict creates interesting panels. E.g. 'deep black exterior, warm amber interior'
what_works.example: Cyberpunk neon batch — the warm/cool contrast was the strongest element

what_works.lesson: Limited palette prompts produce more coherent pages than 'vibrant colors'. 3-4 specific colors > 'colorful'
what_works.example: Risograph batch: 'teal, orange, off-white' produced unified feel across all 6 panels

what_works.lesson: Specifying WHAT the camera sees and FROM WHERE produces better composition than just describing subject
what_works.example: 'dramatic low angle, city visible through window behind him' > 'man at desk at night'

what_works.lesson: AI struggles with hands on keyboards — specify 'hands in soft focus' or 'hands as silhouette' to avoid the uncanny valley
what_works.example: Every batch — hands in sharp focus come out wrong

what_works.lesson: Text in panels (code on screen) always comes out garbled. Either ask for abstracted/glowing code or accept it as texture
what_works.example: Ask for 'lines of glowing text as texture, not readable' rather than real code

what_works.lesson: Moody dark styles all look similar — 'depressed batman' problem. Need to specify vivid color explicitly and name specific saturation anchors
what_works.example: Mike's feedback on batch 1: all looked the same, only cyberpunk had real color

what_works.lesson: The STORY in the caption should be written BEFORE the visual prompt — the narrative informs the image direction, not the reverse
what_works.example: Gibson opening line as caption → the image prompt wrote itself from that

what_works.lesson: Referencing a specific sci-fi/cultural touchstone in the prompt adds depth that pure visual descriptions miss
what_works.example: Matrix digital rain + Spawn cape tendrils → specific visual language with emotional resonance

what_fails: Asking for 'consistent character' across panels — independent generations always drift
Specifying 'realistic hands on keyboard' — always comes out wrong
Asking for readable code on screen — always garbled
Generic style names without artist reference ('noir style', 'comic book style') — too vague
'Vibrant colors' without naming specific colors — AI defaults to muddy or dark
Newspaper/strip style — AI defaults to moody noir regardless of prompt

noir-gold.note: Our original style. Good but generic.

50s-silver-age.note: Retro but fights the subject matter

cyberpunk-neon.note: Too common in AI art. But Mike liked it — has real color.

moebius-ligne-claire.note: AI can't do proper ligne claire

risograph.note: Strongest of batch 1. Plays to AI strengths.

ukiyoe.note: Genuinely colorful, unexpected, has visual identity

newspaper.note: AI ignores the brief, defaults to noir

spawn-matrix-v1.note: Story strong, art 40% there. Missing: competing colors, flowing tendrils, detail density, broken panel borders.

spawn-matrix-v2.note: Generating now — applied: 4-color violence, explicit tendrils breaking borders, 40-hour detail density instruction

next_experiments: Spawn/Matrix with Mike's face as reference — will need photo input
Risograph + color (3 colors but make one vivid)
Ukiyo-e + stronger narrative beats from Gibson/Gibson/Matrix text
Test Imagen 4 vs Gemini 3 pro for comic panels — may produce cleaner results
